contents: Neurological events with tumour necrosis factor alphainhibitors reported to the Food and Drug AdministrationAdverse Event Reporting SystemP. Deepak*, D. J. Stobaugh+, M. Sherid++, H. SifuentesSS & E. D. Ehrenpreis*,+,P*Department of Gastroenterology,Research Institute, NorthShoreUniversity Health System, Evanston,IL, USA.+Center for the Study of ComplexDiseases, Research Institute,NorthShore University HealthSystem,Evanston, IL, USA.++Aspirus Wausau HospitalHospitalists, Wausau, WI, USA.SSDepartment of Gastroenterology andHepatology, Georgia Health SciencesUniversity, Augusta, GA, USA.PUniversity of Chicago, Chicago, IL,USA.Correspondence to:Dr E. D. Ehrenpreis, Center for theStudy of Complex Diseases, ResearchInstitute, NorthShore UniversityHealthSystem, 1001 University Place,Evanston, IL 60201, USA.E-mail: Publication dataSubmitted 19 April 2013First decision 16 May 2013Resubmitted 22 May 2013Accepted 4 June 2013EV Pub Online 26 June 2013SUMMARYBackgroundThe association between inhibition of tumour necrosis factor alpha (TNF-a) andnew onset of neurological adverse events (AEs) is unclear.AimsTo evaluate neurological AEs with TNF-a inhibitors reported to the Food andDrug Administration Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) utilising a standar-dised scoring tool for drug-induced AEs.

MethodsA search of FAERS for neurological AEs (January 1, 2000 to December 31, 2009)reported with infliximab, adalimumab, certolizumab and etanercept was performed.Full-text reports were accessed using the Freedom of Information Act and scoredusing Naranjo score, while accounting for temporal association, previous conclusivereports of the neurological AE with any TNF-a inhibitor, and alternate explana-tions including underlying disease, concomitant medications and comorbidities,such as diabetes mellitus.ResultsThere were 772 reports. Most were in patients who had rheumatoid arthritis (393,50.9%) followed by inflammatory bowel disease (140, 18.1%). No significant differ-ences in age or gender were seen between IBD patients compared with rheumato-logical diseases (P = 0.584 and P = 0.055 respectively). Etanercept was reportedmost (327, 42.4%) followed by infliximab (276, 35.8%) (P = 0.008). Peripheral neu-ropathy was the most common neurological AE (296 reports, 38.3%) followed bycentral nervous system and/or spinal cord demyelination (153 reports, 19.8%).Majority (551, 71.4%) of the reports were of 'possible' AE with the remaining'probable' AE and none identified as 'definite' AE.ConclusionWhile several neurological AEs have been described, definite association betweende novo development of these AEs and exposure to TNF-a inhibitors was notestablished using the Naranjo score.Alimentary Pharmacology and Therapeutics

INTRODUCTIONTumour necrosis factor alpha (TNF-a) inhibitors arecurrently approved in the management of inflammatorybowel disease (IBD) including Crohn's disease (CD),ulcerative colitis (UC) as well as rheumatoid arthritis(RA), ankylosing spondylitis (AS), juvenile rheumatoidarthritis (JRA) and psoriasis. An increased risk of multi-ple sclerosis (MS) exacerbations was first described withthe use of lenercept, an experimental TNF-a inhibitorintroduced to treat MS.1 Since then, several small clinicalseries have reported central nervous system (CNS) demy-elination2 as well as peripheral neuropathies,3 with eta-nercept, infliximab and adalimumab using postmarketingdatabases.It should be noted, however, that IBD may be anindependent risk factor for neurological disease. Neuro-logical abnormalities including cerebrovascular diseaseand peripheral neuropathy have been described in retro-spective case series of patients with IBD. The prevalenceof these ranged from 3% to 19.3% of IBD patients in theera before the use of TNF-a inhibitors, unrelated to aniatrogenic (including medication) or systemic cause.4, 5

Peripheral neuropathies have also been described in pro-spective cohorts in 1.9-13.4% of IBD patients in the erafollowing the introduction of TNF-a inhibitors, afterexcluding causes such as diabetes mellitus and medica-tion exposure.6, 7 Factors postulated in the pathogenesisof neurological abnormalities described with IBD haveincluded micronutrient deficiency,8, 9 and co-occurrenceof inherited thrombophilias with associated prothrom-botic potential secondary to the disease.10 Furthermore,nonbiologic medications including metronidazole11 orcyclosporine12 are also known to produce neurologicalabnormalities.Various neurological manifestations, related to diseaseactivity, have also been described in RA,13 JRA14 andAS.15 Peripheral neuropathy may complicate treatmentwithleflunomide16andhydroxychloroquine.17CNSabnormalities including white matter changes have beenreported following methotrexate treatment, generally incombination with cranial irradiation and at high-doseadministration in cancer chemotherapy.18A variety of neurological complications have beenreportedfollowingtreatmentwithTNF-ainhibi-tors.2, 3, 19 The implication of these reports is that theseoccurrences are due to a direct effect of these medica-tions.Giventhevariousneurologicalabnormalitiesdescribed with autoimmune diseases as well as the vari-ous nonbiological medications used in their treatment,we hypothesised that there is insufficient evidence toestablish a causal relationship between the developmentof CNS and peripheral neuropathies, despite previousreports in postmarketing databases. To evaluate this pos-sibility, we reviewed neurological adverse events (AEs)reported with TNF-a inhibitors to the Food and DrugAdministration Adverse Event Reporting System (FA-ERS) utilising a standardised scoring tool20 to estimatecausality in purported drug-induced AEs.

MATERIALS AND METHODSThe FAERS is a publicly accessible database for postmar-keting safety surveillance for all FDA-approved drugs andtherapeutic biologic agents based on voluntary reportingfrom manufacturers, healthcare professionals, lawyers andconsumers. Individual files were downloaded for the per-iod January 1, 2000 to December 31, 2009 and analysedusing Microsoft Access (Microsoft Corp., Redmond, WA,USA). The database was searched for neurological AEs fol-lowing treatment with the following TNF-a inhibitors: inf-liximab (first approved for CD in 1998), etanercept (firstapproved for RA in 1998), adalimumab (first approved forRA in 2002) and certolizumab (first approved for CD in2008) and their trade names. Golimumab was notincluded in the search as it was only approved in October2009 and the duration since approval was considered tooshort to develop neurological AEs related to its use. Theneurological AE terms used to query the field downloadedto Microsoft Access as well as the classification categoriesare shown in Table S1. Wild cards (*) were used to maxi-mise initial AE report recognition.The Freedom of Information Act was used to obtainfull-length paper charts of all relevant AE reports. Thesewere reviewed individually and analysed to establishauthenticity, identify duplication and eliminate repeti-tion. Reports were perused to confirm the occurrence ofthe neurological AE after the initiation of a TNF-ainhibitors. The cases were then evaluated and informa-tion was collected on age, gender, underlying diseasestate for TNF-a inhibitor usage, dates of TNF-a inhibitorusage and the individual agent implicated in the report.Furthermore, dates of onset and type of neurological AE,along with the objective neurophysiological, pathologicaland imaging test results detailed in the full-text reports,were also noted. The list of concomitant medications foreach report was reviewed extensively to identify medica-tions used in the treatment of the underlying diseasestate(e.g.leflunomide,16hydroxychloroquine17andmethotrexate18) and used in the treatment of comorbidillnesses (e.g. allopurinol,21 acitretin22 and isoniazid23)that may be an alternate explanation for the reported

AE. Significant medical comorbidities (namely diabetesmellitus), which may provide an alternate explanationfor the neurological AE, were also noted.The likelihood of a causal connection between theTNF-a inhibitor and the neurological AEs described ineach report was assessed using the Naranjo score.20 This isa causality assessment score used to determine the likeli-hood of a medication-induced AE. It has been previouslyvalidated, with high within-rater and inter-rater retest reli-ability in addition to consensual, content and concurrentvalidity.20 Scores ranging from 2 to +2 are assigned to 10causality-related questions. Details about the individualquestions used in the scoring tool are available in TableS2. This has been used previously to analyse the associa-tion between exposure to isotretinoin and the develop-ment of IBD.24 For questions with possible ambiguity; wedeveloped rules a priori to prevent subjectivity. Question 1deals with previous conclusive reports on a particular neu-rological AE. Thus, the presence of any published full-textcase report of that particular AE with any of the fourTNF-a inhibitors was scored as +1. Question 2 addressestemporal association of the AE to TNF-a inhibitor expo-sure. Here, all reports were scored as a +2 after assessingfor the occurrence of neurological AE after the initiationof treatment with a TNF-a inhibitor and not being apre-existing condition that worsened or flared with TNF-ainhibitor exposure. For question 5, the presence of analternative cause for the AE was limited to previousdescriptions of the neurological AE secondary to thepatient's underlying autoimmune disease, or with anotherconcomitant medication or comorbidity, such as diabetesmellitus. Finally, question 10 assessed for the presence ofobjective evidence to confirm that the AE actuallyoccurred. Here, reports were scored based on the presenceof positive diagnostic tests considered acceptable for thegiven AE including electromyography, nerve conductionvelocity studies, sensory, motor and visual evoked poten-tial testing, cerebrospinal fluid analysis, nerve biopsies,brain biopsies and/or magnetic resonance imaging of brainand/or spinal cord. The total score from the 10 questionswas computed, and then classified as 'highly probable' (>8points), 'probable' (5-8 points), 'possible' (1-4 points), or'doubtful' (0 points). A compilation of these scores is usedto determine the likelihood that a specific drug treatmenthas a direct causal role in the development of AEs.
RESULTSSeven hundred and ninety-two reports of various neuro-logical AEs were initially identified in the FAERS.Five reports having more than one TNF-a inhibitor

implicated and 15 other reports with more than oneunderlying disease state necessitating TNF-a inhibitorusage were excluded from further analysis. The maxi-mum numbers of reports were in patients with RA (393reports, 50.9%) followed by patient with IBD (140reports, 18.1%) (Table 1). The majority of cases were infemales (479 reports, 62.2%), although two reports weremissinggenderinformation.Nogenderdifferences(Table 1) were seen between patients with IBD com-pared with rheumatological diseases (P = 0.055). Forreports with age information, the mean age of reportedcases was 49.4 (15.6) years across 661 reports (85.6%)wherethisdemographicinformationwasavailable.Again,nosignificantdifferencesinagewereseen(Table 1) between IBD patients compared with rheuma-tological diseases (P = 0.584). Overall, diagnostic testswere performed in 404 patients (52.33%) with reportedneurological AEs from TNF-a inhibitors.Distribution of neurological AEs in IBD as well as inrheumatological diseases (classified by individual TNF-ainhibitor implicated) is shown in Tables 2 and 3 respec-tively. Etanercept was implicated in the majority ofreports (327 reports, 42.4%) followed by infliximab (276reports, 35.8%) (P = 0.008). However, both of theseagents were the earliest approved TNF-a inhibitors(1998) for RA and CD respectively. The most commonAE was peripheral neuropathy, (296 reports, 38.3%),mostly seen with infliximab (120 reports, 40.5%). Nine-ty-seven reports (32.8%) were listed as peripheral neur-opathies unclassified, while 44 reports (14.9%) wereperipheral sensory neuropathies, 27 (9.1%) were motorneuropathies and 19 (6.4%) were sensorimotor neuropa-thies. There were 13 reports (4.4%) of mononeuritis mul-tiplex. We also identified 49 reports (16.6%) of acuteinflammatorydemyelinatingpolyneuropathyand15reports (5.1%) of chronic inflammatory demyelinatingpolyneuropathy (CIDP). Demyelinating events were theotherprominentcategory.Therewere153reports(19.8%) of CNS and/or spinal cord demyelination alongwith 105 reports (13.6%) of optic neuritis and 32 reports(4.2%) listed as 'demyelination' where the affected sitewas not detailed in the full-length report. In addition, 26cases (3.4%) of transverse myelitis were reported.The status of ongoing treatment with the putativeTNF-a inhibitor was available in 264 reports (Table 4).In the reports where the TNF-a inhibitor was continued(29 patients), 10 patients (34.4%) had a full recovery, ofwhich treatment was detailed in three patients (two withfacial palsy and one with optic neuritis). In the 235reports where the TNF-a inhibitor was discontinued, 76

After applying the Naranjo score, there were nocases that met criteria for definite AEs (Table 5). Themajority of reports (551, 71.4%) were scored as 'possible'AEs with scores ranging from 1 to 4. The remainingreports (221 reports, 28.6%) were scored 'probable' AE(score of 5-8) with none identified as 'doubtful' AEs. addition, the majority of cases were scored as 'possible'AEs across the various categories of the neurological AEsas well as for all individual TNF-a inhibitors.DISCUSSIONNeurological AEs represent a serious consequence of drugtherapy with TNF-a inhibitors due to the likelihood ofconsiderable long-term morbidity especially in the con-text of underlying chronic autoimmune diseases. Over a10-year period, 772 distinct reports of neurological AEs,considered secondary to TNF-a inhibitor exposure, weresent to the FAERS of the FDA. When applying a causalityindex to each of these reports, the majority (71.3%) metNaranjo criteria for TNF-a inhibitors being a 'possible'cause of the reported neurological AEs. None of thesereports met 'definite' criteria to link administered TNF-ainhibitors to reported neurological AEs.The BIOEGAS registry of the Spanish Society of Inter-nal Medicine has reported a total of 175 cases of centraldemyelinating processes up to July 2009, as a result ofTNF-a inhibitor therapy. These have included MS andMS-like lesions in 43 patients, optic neuritis in 115patients, eight patients with both MS and optic neuritisand five with other demyelinating lesions.19 Additionally,44 cases of peripheral neuropathies including 20 cases ofGBS, 11 cases of multifocal motor neuropathy with con-duction block, six cases of CIDP, five cases of axonalpolyneuropathy and two cases of Lewis-Summer syn-drome have been reported.

Confounding these reports, it is well known that IBDand other autoimmune disease themselves have beenassociated with demyelinating disorders, even in thepre-TNF-a inhibitor era. A population-based observa-tional study involving 8072 patients with IBD (3879 withUC and 4193 with CD) demonstrated a significantlyhigher prevalence ratio (PR) of MS, as a comorbid diag-nosis, with both UC (PR: 1.81; confidence interval (CI):1.35-2.42) and CD (PR: 1.69; CI: 1.31-2.19).25 A hospi-tal-based case-control study of 155 MS patients similarlydemonstrated a statistically significant coexistence of RAand psoriasis (odds ratio (OR) = 2.96; CI: 1.23-7.66).26Peripheral neuropathies have also been described inboth retrospective and prospective studies involving IBDpatients in the pre-TNF-a inhibitor era. The estimatedprevalence in retrospective studies ranged from 0.9% ina study by Lossos et al.,4 to 2.76% in a series by Elsehetyand Bertorini.5 Two prospective studies, that did notinclude patients on TNF-a inhibitors, established a prev-alence ranging from 1.9% in a Tunisian6 cohort to 13.4%in a Brazilian cohort7. In patients with RA, a prospectiveelectrophysiological evaluation in the pre-TNF-a inhibi-tor era demonstrated findings consistent with sensori-motor neuropathy in 26 of 40 (65%) patients.27 Thepostulated mechanism of peripheral neuropathy attribut-able to chronic inflammatory disorders, such as IBD andRA, is that these patients have high levels of theproinflammatory cytokine TNF-a, resulting in nervedamage.28

See the Terms and Conditions ( on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons LicenseOur study, where the majority of neurological AEreports were in the 'possible' AE category, raises ques-tions regarding the certainty of the association betweenexposure to TNF-a inhibitors and development of theseconditions. Alternative explanations for these AEs devel-oping as a direct result of treatment with TNF-a inhibi-torsincludeunderlyingautoimmunediseases,concomitant medications and the presence of diabetesmellitus or other conditions associated with nerve dam-age. Transverse myelitis was reported in 26 cases; how-ever, this has been described as a first manifestation ofpossible MS in up to 22% of the patients.29 Leucoence-phalopathy was reported in 21 patients, while cerebro-vascular disease was the reported AE in another 13reports.Organicbraindiseaseandencephalopathy,

secondary to RA complicated by vasculitis or rheumatoidnodules, have been previously described.13 Furthermore,white-matter lesions in the brain (without neurologicalsymptoms) have been demonstrated to be significantlymore common in patients with CD (relative risk (RR)2.6, 95% CI: 1.3-5.3) and UC (RR 2.9, 95% CI: 1.3-6.2)compared with healthy age-matched controls.30 Similarly,an increased prevalence of cerebrovascular disease hasbeen described among both RA patients31 (3.02%) andIBD patients4, 5 (0.6-4.7%) in the pre-TNF era. A major-ity of the reports implicated etanercept and infliximab.However,thesetwoTNF-ainhibitorshavebeenapproved for various autoimmune diseases for a longerperiod of time (since 1998) than adalimumab (2002) orcertolizumab (2008), and the greater number of reportsCNS/spinal demyelinationOptic neuritisPeripheral neuropathyFacial palsyMiscellaneous+AdalimumabContinued, n = 14Treatment offered*-2-11Not recovered-27-1Partially recovered--111Recovered-1---Stopped, n = 61Treatment offered*668-3Not recovered571115Partially recovered725-2Recovered34414InfliximabContinued, n = 9Treatment offered*--12-Not recovered--21-Recovered11121Stoppedn = 87Treatment offered*541832Not recovered532134Partially recovered221024Recovered861124EtanerceptContinued, n = 6Treatment offered*-----Not recovered-111-Recovered1-11-Stopped, n = 87Treatment offered*1066412Not recovered1121073Partially recovered73429Recovered55937* Immunosuppressants, intravenous immunoglobulin, corticosteroids, plasmapheresis and anti-viral therapy.

Neurological adverse events with TNF-a inhibitors 13652036, 2013, 4, Downloaded from  by Cochrane Canada Provision, Wiley Online Library on [07/08/2023]. See the Terms and Conditions ( on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons Licensein the FAERS over the time period from 2000 to 2009may be reflective of that.Although our data fail to confirm an absolute associa-tion of neurological AEs and these drugs, previous stud-ies have suggested that TNF-a inhibitors may unmaskpreclinical neurological disease. A prospective study wasconducted by Kotyla et al.,32 involving 28 patients (26women and 2 men) with active RA, with normal baselinenerve conduction amplitudes and velocities. Electromyo-grams after 12 months of infliximab therapy demon-strated increased motoneuronal amplitude in mediannerve (4.93 vs. 8.06 mV) and a decrease in conductionvelocity in the tibial (50.88 vs. 47.08 m/s) and suralnerves (48.73 vs. 44.93 m/s). Hence, it may be prudentto avoid TNF-a inhibitors in patients with pre-existingperipheral neuropathy or demyelination and if absolutelynecessary, seek expert neurological consultation prior toinitiating these agents.33 Given the high prevalence ofMS as a comorbid diagnosis in patients with IBD, RAand psoriasis, assessment for new-onset neurologicalsymptoms at each clinical visit as well as periodic neuro-logical examinations is advised. If any new-onset symp-toms are elicited, discontinuation of the TNF-a inhibitorshould be considered along with a neurological consulta-tion for further assessment and therapy.33 Additionally,caution should be exercised in patients at high risk forMS such as patients with first-degree relatives with MS.26Limitations of the FAERS including both under report-ing34 as well as disproportionate reporting of certain AEswith particular drugs by lawyers35 have been described.Nonetheless, this database has been used effectively to

analyse previous AE reports of TNF-a inhibitors.36 Wehave attempted to compensate for misdiagnoses andreport duplications by reviewing the full-length reports.FAERS does not require definite causation for filing areport.37 However, we have attempted to quantify theassociation of these AEs after exposure to TNF-a inhibi-tors by applying the Naranjo score, a validated probabil-ity scoring tool specific to drug-induced AEs.20To summarise, the strength of association for de novodevelopmentofneurologicalAEswithexposuretoTNF-a inhibitors is not definite after applying probabil-ity scoring for cause and effect in these cases. Futurepopulation-basedresearchiswarrantedtoidentifypatient characteristics that may be associated with sub-clinical neurological diseases in the setting of autoim-mune disease. This will identify a subset that might be atan increased risk of symptomatic neurological diseasewhen using TNF-a inhibitors.AUTHORSHIPGuarantor of the article: Dr Eli D.
